description.text:
## Description

I figured since @benniekiss is now churning out quite some code, it makes sense for me to also switch to PRs for somewhat bigger changes to avoid issues like the footnote mayhem recently.

This PR refactors the md2html function and will – once it's done – enable us to produce more complete HTML, which primarily includes generating a bibliography for the print preview.

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->

* Refactored the MD2HTML to return a Promise
* Unify option API for the function
* Adapt existing places across the codebase to use this new signature
* TODO: Enable bibliography generation

## Additional information

Tested on: macOS 26 Tahoe

## Description This PR fully rewrites the citation parser to now actually add appropriate SyntaxNodes into the parse tree that can in the future be used by various parts in the code to extract and render citations. "Why the f\*\*\* would you rewrite a perfectly working system?!" Well, because I had my gripes with the way citations were handled up until now. To begin with, the existing citation parser still stems from CodeMirror 5 days, when adding new syntax nodes was not straight forward. It cuts many edges, is slow, and mounts single, monolithic citation nodes into the tree with no proper subdivision of the various citation elements. The new Markdown parser allows for a better inline parser for adding additional syntax elements, and so having a more accurate and up-to-date parser has a ton of benefits, including, but not limited to: * Now all elements of citations are mounted as proper SyntaxNodes. This includes the citation formatting characters (brackets, the @-sign), prefix and suffix, citekey, and locator. * This means that CSL Citation Items (which are required by citeproc.js to prerender citations and construct bibliographies) can now be fully constructed by only looking at the parse trees -- no more additional regular expression wizardry required. ~~(This is still todo)~~ * In addition, I now enabled the parser to finally pick up on weird citekeys and complex locators that Pandoc allows using bracketed syntax (e.g., `@{a complex citekey with otherwise unallowed characters}`) * Every piece of a citation can now be individually styled, if one so wishes (see the screenshots below for some examples that I used during testing) * This further cements the parse tree as the single source of truth for information about a document. No more additional parsing required, because citations are no longer mounted as individual, monolithic syntax nodes. ~~However, this still needs to be implemented in some parts of the application (hence, only a draft PR).~~ * This, incidentally, also allows users to turn off citation parsing and still detect whether a citation looks correct. * Furthermore, the previous method of citation parsing was computationally very intensive, because many parts of the document had to be parsed over and over to find citations with complex regular expressions and in general some pretty complex computations. Now, it's as fast as it can practically get. ## Changes The changes are huge, but from a theoretical standpoint not that big. Citations are now parsed using a relatively basic character-based parser that simply moves through the inline context and tries to parse a citation until it finds a stop condition. I additionally took the liberty of aggressively checking for potential stop-conditions to abort parsing as early as possible once a citation can be guaranteed to be invalid. This makes the parser much more efficient, and also aligns it more with the intentions of Marijn's parser that is intended to be run character for character. ## Additional information Some screenshots demonstrating the individual syntax nodes and showing a few edge cases: I have created those screenshots with the following temporary rules in the Berlin theme: ```ts '.cm-citation': { textDecoration: 'underline' }, '.cm-citation-mark': { fontFamily: 'monospace', color: '#6633ff' }, '.cm-citation-prefix': { color: 'var(--grey-1)' }, '.cm-citation-suppress-author-flag': { color: 'red' }, '.cm-citation-at-sign': { fontFamily: 'monospace' }, '.cm-citation-citekey': { color: 'var(--green-0)' }, '.cm-citation-locator': { fontFamily: 'monospace', color: 'red' }, '.cm-citation-suffix': { color: 'var(--grey-1)' }, ``` ## TODOs There are a few remaining todos: - [x] Utilize the parse tree information to render citations in the citation renderer - [x] Utilize the parse tree information to generate citations in the MarkdownAST - [x] Fully sherlock and remove the old citation parser, as it is no longer needed *** Tested on: macOS Tahoe 26

## Description

This is the PR I'll continuisly keep updated in order to close #3063

## Changes

This PR has one job: To get rid of the `globals` and side-effects pertaining to these. In order to do these, the following changes have been made (checkboxes are ticked):

- [x] Create an `AppServiceContainer` which serves as a singleton class that can inject service providers into other service providers
- [x] Move all provider instantiations into the app service container which is launched and shut down just like the service providers before in the lifecycle functions
- [x] Inject providers on a need-be basis to the providers, that is: all receive an instance of the logger, the rest depending on their needs
- [x] Refactored the `WindowManager` into the `WindowProvider`
- [x] Fixed a race condition in which the window provider and the tray provider required each other simultaneously
- [x] Moved the loading of the language data into the translation provider, where it should've been for years now
- [x] Refactor the `DocumentManager` into the `DocumentProvider`
- [x] Refactor the `Zettlr` class into a `CommandProvider`
- [x] Completely remove all `globals` in the main process
- [x] Clean up the `global.d.ts` and remove any provider typings as possible, since the types are now propagated correctly via dependency injection
- [x] Clean the absurd dependency hell that has just unearthed in the FSAL
- [ ] Find a solution to providing the language data via the `globals`, since that is something I have not yet figured out without having every file require the translation provider (or is that feasible?)
- [x] The md2html converter depends on the citation provider, we need to inject that both in main and in the renderer

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->

I did some quick testing and thus far the app starts without all too much hassle. However, I still don't trust the linter via ESLint vis-a-vis VSCode's linting vis-a-vis the typechecking during builds, since they give me mixed messages. Feel free to have additional looks at this.

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS 12 (Monterey), arm64 arch, but this PR should be platform independent

***

CC @tobiasdiez @kyaso — this PR is not yet done so you don't have to do any deep code review just now. Just feel free to keep an eye out on this PR, and feel free to suggest improvements if you see I'm running into the wrong direction somewhere.

closed_issues.summary: [DEVELOP] Migrate main modules to service providers

description.text:
I'm opening this issue to have a communication channel with the rest of the community regarding a large refactoring I've begun.

Until now, there was this distinction between `modules` and `service providers`, where modules were roughly equivalent to simple Javascript modules (i.e. you have a folder with an index and in there resides the module) and service providers were modules which did not expose any access to other parts of the code. The only code which had direct access to the service providers is the lifecycle hook which boots them up and shuts them down. Access to the internal state of these modules was always facilitated using the `global` object (inside the main process) and the `broadcastIpcMessage` function (towards the renderer processes).

While that was a cool thing, the main reason I used the `global` object was both because module support in an early version of Node was not that good and simply because I honestly didn't understand how modules work.

Over the past two years, however, the whole setup of the app has changed quite considerably. That has had several reasons:

* First, I should name the efforts by the Electron team to mitigate security issues with Electron apps, among these deactivation of node integration, context isolation, and other features which were slowly included. This means that the renderers are now fully autonomous and we could – if we provide the correct APIs – just take some of the renderer process and display it as-is on a website.
* Then, @tobiasdiez has introduced TypeScript to the codebase, and, alongside with that, webpack, electron-forge and thus a host of new possibilities. Ever since his large PR was merged, the files are now combined, minimized and otherwise optimized.
* Furthermore, I lost my fear that something bad could happen in the main process. Or, rather: I now know how to keep code safe without locking everything up.

Additionally to these reasons, there are now new demands for the application: Progress is halting, mainly because code that I would like to implement doesn't have access to some of the codebase which is necessary to implement this simple. As a very palpable example, take the demand for a network visualization of the files: While we now do have a link provider, that link provider is currently unable to resolve the outlinks into correct path names. The functionality for that, however, is already there: the `findExact` method of the FSAL. However, since that is only accessible by the main Zettlr object, the link provider has no means to resolve those links.

In fact, the main Zettlr class is the last remnant from the early versions prior to 1.0 where I organized everything OOP, an hommage to my background with Laravel and PHP in general, where OOP is much more prevalent than in the JavaScript world.

Given that background, here's what I am currently trying to do:

1. Refactor the service providers from classes to functional components which maintain a module-wide state and expose functions which allow limited access to that state in a safe manner. This will not only remove our reliance on the global object, but additionally make typing much easier, since we can simply type the functions and thus remove a lot of boilerplate from the type files (which I already migrated into a new `types` directory)
2. Convert the FSAL, the DocumentManager, and the Importer and Exporter to service providers, that is: Functional modules residing on the top level of the app. This will then additionally end the distinction between service providers and modules: The main process will then only have service providers. The renderers will still have modules, but that is because there are multiple of them, and for example the Markdown editor is something that's needed in several places
3. After this has been done, one of my next goals would be if I can safely remove the `zettlr.ts` class file. I don't yet know how to do this the most proper way, but I'm sure after there is no more state which the class manages a few avenues will open up.

The benefits of this whole endeavor will be that the complexity of the main process is radically reduced and we can finally begin to implement a few other neat things such as the typing of IPC modules which Tobias has already begun working on. Furthermore, this will make it easier for us to slowly migrate the whole preload module towards a way that offers a simple API. This will in turn make it easy to – some day – take the main window code, move it to an external repository, and from there on build a web application (PWA) of Zettlr, which utilizes the code in two places: The desktop app and said web app.

***

In the end, I think this is simply the logical conclusion of going from "I have no idea what I'm doing" towards someone who actually knows what a desktop app needs and how to properly structure it. All in all, it's just another technical debt in the wall!

CC because you seem to be reasonbly active in making PRs and/or thus might be interested to know about this: @kyaso @derwok @Kangie @newhinton
